mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: ACID: Always Come In Drinking (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)

mnemonics.title: Transaction Roles
mnemonics.content: TM (Transaction Manager) coordinates, RM (Resource Manager) responds

acronyms.usage: A protocol ensuring atomicity in distributed transactions with Prepare and Commit phases
Non-blocking commit protocol with CanCommit, PreCommit, and DoCommit phases
Orchestrates the distributed transaction across multiple resources
Manages the actual data resources like databases participating in the transaction
Four properties that guarantee reliable transaction processing
Theorem describing trade-offs in distributed systems design

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Coordinator at top, Participants at bottom. Phase 1: Coordinator → Participants (Prepare), Participants → Coordinator (Vote Yes/No). Phase 2: Coordinator → Participants (Commit/Abort), Participants acknowledge.
Add an intermediate 'PreCommit' bubble between Prepare and Commit. This ensures participants know others are ready before actual commit.
Draw an inverted tree: Parent at top, children below. Parent controls children. Show arrows from parent to children showing control flow.
Draw participant in 'wait' state after Prepare, with a dashed line to failed coordinator. Show participant stuck waiting.

analogies.analogy: Like a group project: Phase 1: Team lead asks everyone if ready (Prepare), Phase 2: If all say yes, lead says 'Submit' (Commit), otherwise 'Don't submit' (Abort).
Like a wedding planner coordinating venues, caterer, and florist - all must agree and complete for the wedding to happen.
Like a company with departments (parent) and teams (children). Each team can complete their task, but parent oversees the whole project.
Like a series of LinkedIn recommendations: if someone removes their recommendation, you can ask the previous person to remove theirs (compensating action).
